seo.title: Active vs Passive Funds: The Real Answer for Indian Investors
seo.description: Active vs Passive investing is one of the biggest debates in mutual funds. But is there one universal answer? In this clip, Anupam Tiwari, Head of

takeaways.text: Active versus passive is one of the biggest debates in mutual funds, but this episode argues there is no single universal answer for every investor.
Anupam Tiwari, Head of Equities at Groww Mutual Fund, explains why the rise of passive investing in the US may not map directly onto India.
The US market has narrowed over time, with fewer listed companies and growth concentrated in a handful of large winners, which is friendlier ground for passive.
India's listed universe is still expanding, especially beyond large caps, giving active fund managers a broader opportunity set to work with.
The real answer may depend less on active or passive as a slogan and more on where you are investing: large cap, mid cap, or small cap.

fork.decision: Should Indian investors go active or passive?

points.text: Passive tends to work well when a market is narrow, with growth concentrated in a handful of large winners, as has happened in the US.
In large caps, where the opportunity set is more concentrated, tracking the index at low cost is a reasonable default.
India's listed universe is still expanding, especially beyond large caps, so the opportunity set for managers stays broad.
Active can still create alpha when the opportunity universe is broad, which is where mid and small caps sit.

fork.pick: Do not answer this once for your whole portfolio. Match the choice to the cap: in large caps, where the field is narrow, low-cost passive is a fair default, while in mid and small caps, where India's listed universe is still widening, an active manager has real room to add value.
